title: Markets recalibrate amid shifting currents
summary: The BIS Quarterly Review for March 2026 documents significant shifts in global financial markets during the review period, with investors rotating away from previously high-performing US large cap and growth stocks toward European, Japanese, and emerging market equities. While index-level volatility rose modestly, individual stock volatility increased substantially more. The Middle East conflict in early March triggered reversals in several trends, pushing oil and gas prices higher, causing the US dollar to reverse its depreciation, and pressuring equity gains across European and Asian markets. Credit markets remained broadly stable, though leveraged loans and private credit faced headwinds.
key_points: Global equity rotation: investors moved away from US large cap and growth stocks toward European, Japanese, and emerging market equities; value and small cap outperformed
Index volatility masked sharper individual stock volatility; dispersion between individual stock moves increased significantly
US dollar initially depreciated (typical risk-on behavior) but Swiss franc simultaneously appreciated (risk-off hallmark); Middle East escalation in early March reversed USD depreciation
Gold and silver surged early 2026 but rally ended abruptly in late January due to leveraged position unwinds; precious metals later traded more like risk assets than safe havens
Sovereign bond markets diverged: long-term yields rose sharply in Japan while US and euro area moved sideways; euro area sovereign spreads widened after Middle East conflict
Private credit with software sector exposures faced investor redemptions amid concerns over AI-driven disruption to software companies
High valuations raised questions about technology sector sustainability; P/E ratios for broader market approached dotcom bubble levels though M7 ratios were comparatively lower
Emerging market assets initially rallied on weaker dollar and carry trades but momentum waned as geopolitical tensions escalated
why_it_matters: This BIS Quarterly Review provides comprehensive documentation of cross-asset market dynamics during a period of rotation and elevated uncertainty. Understanding these structural shifts—particularly the rotation away from concentrated US tech exposure, the divergence between index and stock-level volatility, and the mixed FX signals—is essential for portfolio construction and risk management. The document captures how geopolitical tensions disrupted otherwise resilient risk appetite and provides context for positioning across equities, fixed income, FX, and alternatives.
macro_context: Inflation expectations edged up during the review period, leading investors to revise expectations of monetary policy rates upward and push back expected timing of US rate cuts. Higher expected inflation and geopolitical tensions drove bond yields higher and caused mild curve flattening, reversing prior steepening. The macro context is one of recalibration: resilient corporate fundamentals and solid macroeconomic data releases broadly supported risk assets, but valuation concerns, AI spending uncertainty, and geopolitical disruptions created bouts of volatility and selective risk-taking.
market_structure_context: The document highlights several market structure dynamics: (1) Dispersion between index-level and individual stock volatility suggests increased stock-picking opportunities but also challenges for passive strategies; (2) The concentration risk of M7 stocks (nearly 35% of S&P 500) amplifying index moves when these stocks decline; (3) Shifts in equity fund flows from US toward EMEs and other advanced economies; (4) Credit market stability masking differences across segments, with leveraged loans and private credit facing headwinds; (5) Sustained investor demand for downside protection (volatility hedging) even during periods of relative calm.
important_numbers: near dotcom bubble levels (P/E ratios for broader market)
notes: BIS Quarterly Review publication from March 2026 represents a high-quality, authoritative source on global financial market developments. The document is well-sourced and provides granular analysis of market structure dynamics including rotation patterns, volatility dispersion, and cross-asset correlations. Source is recent (within 90 days of TODAY) and highly relevant to market structure analysis. Document refers to Box A (AI hyperscalers) and Box B (private credit software exposures) that appear to contain additional relevant detail but were not included in the truncated text. Key takeaways section and multiple graphs referenced (Graph 1.A, 1.B, 1.C, 2.A, 2.B, 2.C) provide additional structure not captured in this extraction.
